title: How the US-Iran war is affecting the global energy transition
gist: The 2026 US-Iran war and Hormuz crisis are a short-term brake on the energy transition but strengthen the long-term security case for clean energy; the 2026 Energy Transition Index shows transition readiness falling for the first time in over a decade, with policy credibility emerging as the new risk premium driving cost-of-capital divergence.

insights.claim: The 2026 Energy Transition Index, tracking 120 countries across 44 indicators, finds transition readiness fell this year for the first time in over a decade, with four of five enabling dimensions declining simultaneously.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: A broad deterioration in transition enablers signals slowing clean-energy deployment momentum globally; watch capex revisions.
insights.quote: The 2026 Energy Transition Index, which tracks 120 countries across 44 indicators, finds that transition readiness fell this year, with four of five enabling dimensions declining simultaneously.
insights.locator: For the first time in over a decade

insights.claim: Overall energy transition progress in 2026 was near-flat at +0.03%, while readiness conditions (policy stability, investment access, innovation capacity and infrastructure) weakened together.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Flat headline progress masks eroding foundations, implying future deceleration risk for renewables buildout.
insights.quote: Overall, the progress was near-flat (+0.03%); however, the conditions that make future progress possible, essentially the readiness for the transition, policy stability, investment access, innovation capacity and infrastructure, have weakened together.
insights.locator: This year's index, built on pre-crisis data

insights.claim: Energy security was the only system performance dimension to decline in 2026, falling 0.9%, driven by weaker supply diversification (-0.7%) and a sharp 3.0% drop in reliability.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Reliability deterioration favors investment in grid resilience, storage and domestic supply.
insights.quote: falling 0.9%, driven by weaker supply diversification (-0.7%) and a sharp drop in reliability (-3.0%).

insights.claim: Renewables and nuclear reached 42% of global electricity generation in 2026, with nearly 800 gigawatts of renewable capacity added and energy efficiency improving across 92 economies.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Clean generation share past 40% confirms structural demand even amid the security shock; supports renewable supply-chain plays.
insights.quote: Renewables and nuclear reached 42% of global electricity generation. Nearly 800 gigawatts of renewable capacity were added. Energy efficiency improved across 92 economies.
insights.locator: What the Energy Transition Index shows on current performance

insights.claim: ASEAN alone is absorbing an estimated $3.4 billion per month in excess energy costs from the crisis, while India has recommissioned coal, South Korea has lifted coal generation restrictions, and the Philippines has declared a national energy emergency.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Near-term coal demand rebound and fiscal strain in emerging Asia; bullish thermal coal short-term, watch emerging-market sovereign stress.
insights.quote: The Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) alone is absorbing an estimated $3.4 billion per month in excess energy costs. India has recommissioned coal. South Korea has lifted coal generation restrictions. The Philippines has declared a national energy emergency.
insights.locator: This crisis will put affordability gains under sustained pressure

insights.claim: The article argues advanced economies can absorb higher energy costs through strategic reserves and fiscal headroom while many emerging markets must simultaneously manage energy access, fiscal stability and transition investment with less room.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Energy shock widens the divide between DM and EM energy resilience; positions EM transitions as more fragile.
insights.locator: This crisis will put affordability gains under sustained pressure

insights.claim: The ASEAN Power Grid initiative, backed by Asian Development Bank and World Bank financing, could mobilize around $760 billion in generation and transmission investment by 2045, enabling resource sharing and a buffer against single-route exposure.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Large multi-decade grid capex pipeline in Southeast Asia; opportunity for transmission and grid-equipment suppliers.
insights.quote: The ASEAN Power Grid initiative, which is now backed by financing from the Asian Development Bank and the World Bank, could mobilize around $760 billion in generation and transmission investment by 2045
insights.locator: ASEAN is moving down the second path

insights.claim: Japan's Asia Zero Emission Community framework, originally designed for clean energy transition, is being repurposed as a regional energy security mechanism, with Japan committing financing to help Asian economies secure supply and build reserve capacity.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Climate frameworks pivoting to security framing draws fresh state financing; cross-border power flows become security plays.
insights.quote: Cross-border power flows are no longer purely an efficiency measure; they are a security strategy.
insights.locator: ASEAN is moving down the second path

themes.theme: Signals to watch: renewables-as-security, LNG, policy credibility

insights.claim: The article argues the US-Iran crisis is reframing renewables from a cost-and-sustainability case to an industrial security asset, with China accelerating hydrogen use from local wind and solar and India and the EU reviewing biofuel and clean-fuel mandates through a security lens.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Security framing could unlock procurement and policy tailwinds for domestic renewables and clean fuels.
insights.quote: clean energy is increasingly framed as an industrial security asset, and procurement decisions are beginning to follow.

insights.claim: Around 19% of global LNG trade transits the Strait of Hormuz with almost 90% bound for Asia, and Japan and Korea are responding by locking in long-term contracts from additional sources while others look past gas entirely.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Watch the next wave of Asian LNG contracts and whether gas demand projections through 2035 are revised down; chokepoint risk supports non-Hormuz LNG suppliers.
insights.quote: Around 19% of global LNG trade transits the Strait of Hormuz, with almost 90% bound for Asia.
insights.locator: 2. How the 'transition fuel' narrative sustains under pressure

insights.claim: The 2026 Energy Transition Index shows cost-of-capital divergence between markets is set to widen through 2026 and 2027 if recent trends continue, making policy credibility the binding constraint and new risk premium for the next phase of the transition.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Capital will concentrate in jurisdictions with stable, predictable energy policy; policy-credibility screening becomes an investment edge.
insights.quote: if recent trends continue, cost-of-capital divergence between markets is set to widen through 2026 and 2027.
insights.locator: 3. Whether policy credibility becomes the new risk premium

insights.claim: The article cites the EU's REPowerEU, set up to diversify supply after the Ukraine-Russia conflict, as evidence that crisis-driven energy-security logic tends to persist even after the original crisis fades.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Crisis-induced diversification spending is sticky, implying durable structural demand for diversified clean supply post-crisis.
insights.quote: this logic, once adopted, tends to hold even as the original crisis fades.
insights.locator: ASEAN is moving down the second path
